text: Hello friends. This is a case of large dermoid cyst.
This is approximately twenty year old girl unmarried and she has one mole around the umbilicus that is normal.
The surgeon inserts a supraumbilical port to access the abdominal cavity, preparing to address a large dermoid cyst located on the right side.
The surgeon introduces a surgical instrument approximately five centimeters above the umbilicus, preparing for the procedure on the large dermoid cyst.
The surgeon uses an irrigation and suction device to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris from the area surrounding the dermoid cyst.
The surgeon performs the hanging drop test, where fluid is visibly sucked into the trocar, indicating proper placement within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs insufflation using a preset pressure of fifteen, with the actual pressure gradually increasing from single digits. The flow rate is set at one liter per minute.
The surgeon introduces a trocar after insufflating two liters of gas, positioning it in the supraumbilical area to access the surgical site with a large right-sided dermoid cyst.
And this is again supramolecule. And this is baseball diamond concept. Index finger is kept over the target.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of two ports in the snuff box region, aligning them along the dermo-axis for the procedure.
Now of course we are not using uterine manipulator in this girl.
The surgeon introduces an Ethicon endo catch bag into the surgical field, opening it to contain the specimen and minimize spillage during the cystectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs aspiration of a large cyst using a syringe, initially collecting 5 to 10 ml of oily fluid for cytology, indicating the fatty content of the dermoid cyst.
The surgeon removes the aspiration needle and enlarges the incision slightly before using suction to evacuate the cyst contents.
The surgeon positions the endo bag through the contralateral port, utilizing two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port for the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to enlarge the incision slightly, preparing for further surgical steps.

A 5mm suction device is introduced into the surgical field, rapidly aspirating the oily fluid content from the cyst. The suction tubing visibly carries the fluid away, demonstrating the efficient removal of the cyst's contents.
The surgeon observes the collapse of the cyst following aspiration, with the cyst walls visibly shrinking as the fluid is removed.
A small amount of fluid spills outside the surgical site, and the surgeon promptly uses a suction device to remove the spilled fluid.
The surgeon begins stripping the inner cystic layer after fluid has been sucked out, preparing for enucleation of the cyst. Ectodermal contents such as hair, tooth, or nail remain unsucked.

The surgeon identifies another follicular cyst and demonstrates the separation of the inner cystic layer from the outer ovarian cortex. The cystic layer is carefully dissected away from the ovarian cortex, emphasizing the need for gentle manipulation to achieve a clear tissue plane.
Dermoid cyst give you relatively better plane than endometrioma because it is congenital so it has a good plane compared to the endometrioma.
The surgeon identifies the cystic layer and gently separates it from the ovarian cortex, observing the gradual separation of these tissue layers.
The surgeon demonstrates ambidextrous technique, alternating between using the left instrument to hold the cyst and the right instrument to strip it, and vice versa.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector in one hand and a semi-traumatic grasper in the other to carefully separate the cystic layer from the ovarian cortex, ensuring a gentle and precise dissection.

The surgeon strips the cyst from the ovarian cortex, revealing a good plane that allows for easy separation. Minor oozing is visible, which is managed with irrigation and suction as the last part of the cyst is extracted.
The surgeon visually identifies the cyst on the right side and the remaining ovary on the left side, noting that the ovary is detached from the cyst.
The surgeon places the excised cyst into an endo bag and closes the mouth of the bag using a purse string technique.
The cyst is visibly positioned in the surgical field, appearing well-placed after dissection.

The surgeon closes the mouth of the endo bag using a metal ring, which is cut out from the bag. The purse string is applied to secure the closure. The cyst is then pulled out of the surgical site with slight dilation using fingers.
The surgeon completes the extraction of the cyst from the endo bag, ensuring the cyst is fully removed from the surgical field.
This was just a simple case of Thermo-Acid System.
